does: This paper presents a gradient-free machine unlearning algorithm that removes specific classes from trained models without full retraining. The method operates by decomposing model activations into Retain and Forget spaces using Singular Value Decomposition on layerwise activations from small sample subsets. It identifies and removes shared information between these spaces to isolate class-discriminatory features, then updates model weights to suppress these features from activation spaces. The approach is evaluated on ImageNet using Vision Transformers, achieving class removal while maintaining approximately 98.5% accuracy on retained classes, demonstrating computational efficiency compared to retraining or iterative finetuning approaches.
connects: This work addresses the machine unlearning problem that has gained prominence alongside regulatory requirements like GDPR and heightened privacy concerns. It builds on prior research distinguishing between sample-level and class-level unlearning, extending beyond gradient-based methods that typically require backpropagation or access to training data. The paper connects to literature on feature decomposition and activation space analysis in neural networks, leveraging SVD-based techniques similar to those used in model compression and interpretability. It responds to computational bottlenecks in existing unlearning approaches that either retrain from scratch or perform multiple finetuning iterations, positioning itself as an efficient alternative to these resource-intensive methods.
enables: This work opens directions for practical deployment of machine unlearning in resource-constrained settings where retraining is prohibitive. The gradient-free approach enables efficient handling of deletion requests in production systems with minimal computational overhead. Future work could extend the method to sample-level unlearning, combine it with other privacy techniques for stronger guarantees, or apply activation space decomposition to other model architectures and domains beyond vision. The efficiency gains suggest potential for real-time unlearning services and could inform privacy-preserving deployment of large foundation models where selective forgetting becomes operationally feasible rather than economically prohibitive.
